title: Sports at Home
subtitle: Guided, evidence-based training with the furniture and objects you already own — built for fragile and deconditioned users, and scalable for the fit.
disclaimer: Not medical advice. Home training with household objects is a way to be active, not a treatment or cure for any condition. If you are older, pregnant, deconditioned, or have a medical condition, get clinician clearance (PAR-Q+ screening) before starting. Objects are not calibrated weights — work to comfortable effort, not to failure. Stop and seek care for chest pain or pressure, severe or unusual breathlessness, dizziness or faintness, palpitations, or new or worsening joint pain.

premise.summary: Three independent lines of evidence make furniture-and-objects training defensible: equipment type is not a primary driver of resistance-training outcomes; home delivery is non-inferior to centre delivery for the outcomes studied; and even very small movement doses carry benefit.

points.claim: The training is the active ingredient, not the equipment — moving from no resistance training to any is the biggest gain, and equipment type and program complexity do not consistently change outcomes.
Home delivery holds up even in cardiac rehabilitation, where home- and centre-based programs gave similar benefits for the outcomes studied.
Short accumulated bouts count: ~4.4 min/day of vigorous lifestyle activity tracked with 26-34% lower mortality in non-exercisers, and structured exercise snacks improve fitness.

levels.name: Seated / supported
levels.desc: All work seated or holding a chair, counter, or wall. Safest start for the most deconditioned, for symptom days, and for anyone's first session.

levels.name: Standing, supported to free
levels.desc: Bodyweight patterns with a hand on support, progressing to unassisted, plus light external load (water bottles). Builds standing tolerance, balance, and the squat/hinge/push/pull foundations.

levels.name: Loaded home strength
levels.desc: Backpack- and bottle-loaded compound patterns with managed progression; balance and functional work retained.

levels.name: Conditioning / advanced
levels.desc: Higher volume, circuits, stair and tempo work, unilateral and power-biased variations for fit users wanting a real challenge.

programs.name: Fragile / deconditioned track
programs.desc: Leads with seated and supported tiers and ALWAYS pairs strength with balance and functional tasks. Balance/functional exercise is the active ingredient for falls reduction; resistance training alone is uncertain for falls. A chair is present every session.

programs.name: Overweight track (function-framed)
programs.desc: Same ladder with joint-sparing variations (chair/wall support, incline push-ups, supported squats early). Framed around strength, stamina, and how you feel — not weight. No calorie or weight targets.

programs.name: Chronic-condition adjunct packs (cleared)
programs.desc: Cleared, adjunct-framed presets for type 2 diabetes (plus break-up-sitting prompts), hypertension (with isometric/breathing guards), knee osteoarthritis, and cancer survivorship. Adjunct to medical care, never a replacement.

programs.name: Experienced / fit track
programs.desc: Loaded and conditioning tiers, progressing by tempo, range, unilateral loading, and density. Honest about the absolute-load ceiling of household objects.

objects.name: Sturdy chair (no wheels, against a wall)
objects.safety: Confirm it cannot slide or tip; back it to a wall.

objects.name: Wall
objects.role: Isometric brace, push surface, balance aid

objects.name: Water bottles / jugs (sealed)
objects.load_logic: Volume to mass: 1 L is about 1 kg; fill to titrate.
objects.safety: Check the seal; start light; keep a neutral wrist.

objects.name: Backpack (loaded with books/bottles)
objects.role: Trunk-proximal load for squats, hinges, carries
objects.load_logic: Weigh on a scale if possible; otherwise count contents.
objects.safety: Pack the load high and tight; do not round the back.

objects.name: Bath / beach towel
objects.safety: Use sliders on hard floor only.

objects.name: Stairs / a single step
objects.role: Aerobic snack, power, calf and quad loading
objects.safety: Keep a rail within reach; one stair for beginners.

objects.name: Countertop / heavy table edge
objects.role: High brace for supported standing work

objects.name: Broom / mop handle
objects.safety: Keep it light; this is pattern practice, not load.

movement_patterns.name: Sit-to-stand / squat
movement_patterns.progression: chair entry, then supported bodyweight, then backpack-loaded, then unilateral
movement_patterns.note: The single most functional pattern; 30-second sit-to-stand also works as a home capacity check.

movement_patterns.name: Hip hinge
movement_patterns.progression: broom-handle groove, then bodyweight, then backpack hinge, then single-leg
movement_patterns.note: Teach the pattern before loading it to protect the back.

movement_patterns.name: Push (horizontal / incline)
movement_patterns.progression: wall, then counter, then chair-incline, then floor
movement_patterns.note: Joint-friendly entry for overweight and deconditioned users.

movement_patterns.name: Pull
movement_patterns.progression: towel self-isometric and bottle rows, then backpack rows, then table rows
movement_patterns.note: The hardest pattern to load at home; named honestly.

movement_patterns.name: Carry / loaded gait
movement_patterns.note: High transfer to daily life; gentle on joints; scales by fill level.

movement_patterns.name: Balance / gait
movement_patterns.progression: supported single-leg stance, then tandem stand, then reaching and stepping tasks
movement_patterns.note: The falls-prevention core for fragile users.

movement_patterns.name: Aerobic snacks
movement_patterns.progression: marching, then sit-to-stand intervals, then stairs

movement_patterns.name: Isometric holds (plank / wall / anti-rotation)
movement_patterns.progression: shorter holds, then longer holds, then a harder lever or longer body line
movement_patterns.note: Trunk and postural endurance; keep breathing throughout — never hold your breath.

screening.model: PAR-Q+ (international standard for self-administered pre-participation screening)
screening.logic: Seven general questions; if all answers are no, self-clear for unrestricted activity; if any is yes, ask targeted follow-up; if follow-up flags remain, route to a qualified professional or ePARmed-X+.
rules: The app never overrides a 'see a professional' outcome.
Re-screen on any new condition, symptom, pregnancy, injury, or long lay-off.
Cardiac disease, recent cardiac events, or uncontrolled symptoms route to clinical care, not to a tier.

intensity.summary: Because objects are uncalibrated, dose is controlled by perception and reserve, not by assumed kilograms.

signals.name: Rate of perceived exertion (RPE)
signals.use: Primary intensity signal on a simple 0-10 scale with plain talk-anchors.

signals.name: Repetitions in reserve (RIR)
signals.use: Fragile/beginners ~3-4 RIR; general ~2-3 RIR. Training to failure is not required for benefit.

signals.name: Talk test
signals.use: Gates aerobic snacks: full sentences = light; short phrases = vigorous band.

signals.name: Progression
signals.use: Change one variable at a time (reps, then range, then tempo, then unilateral, then heavier object); technique quality is the gate; deload by dropping a tier on symptom days.

benefits.detail: Progressive resistance training improves strength, hypertrophy, power, and function; the dominant determinant is doing it at all, not the equipment. In older adults it improves walking, stair climbing, and chair rises.

Balance and functional exercise cuts the rate of falls ~24% (high-certainty); multi-component programs ~34%. Resistance training ALONE is uncertain for falls — so balance content is mandatory in the fragile track.

All exercise modes lower resting BP; isometric training is most effective (~8.2/4 mmHg), and the wall squat ranked the single most effective sub-mode for systolic BP. Caveat: high-intensity static holds with breath-holding can transiently raise BP during the effort.

Regular activity improves blood-glucose control and cardiovascular risk; breaking up sitting every 30 minutes with brief light activity (e.g. leg extensions, overhead arm stretches) helps glucose handling. Adjunct to care.
Land-based exercise gives moderate immediate reduction in pain (~12/100) and improved function (~10/100), sustained 2-6 months; favour supported, controlled-range variations.

Exercise is generally safe (clearance advised in specific situations) and improves anxiety, depressive symptoms, fatigue, physical function, and quality of life; avoid inactivity. Adjunct to oncology care.

Exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation reduces MI risk and hospitalisation and likely slightly reduces mortality, with home delivery non-inferior to centre delivery for studied outcomes. Cited to justify home delivery and set the boundary — the app is NOT cardiac rehab.
In non-exercisers, ~4.4 min/day of vigorous lifestyle activity tracked with 26-30% lower all-cause/cancer and 32-34% lower CVD mortality, near-linearly. Observational, but robust.

benefits.outcome: Cardiorespiratory fitness from short home bouts
benefits.detail: Structured exercise snacks improve cardiorespiratory fitness (moderate certainty), feasible across mixed populations. Effects on muscular endurance and broader outcomes are less certain.

not_supported: This is not a weight-loss program and must not be framed as one — exercise alone produces modest weight change; benefits accrue largely independent of the scale. The app measures strength, stamina, balance, and adherence, not weight, and prescribes no calorie or weight targets.
This is not cardiac rehabilitation, disease treatment, or physiotherapy — higher-risk cardiac disease needs supervised, risk-stratified care; flagged users are routed out.
Objects are not calibrated weights — the app cannot tell you an exact load from a 'full backpack'; intensity is governed by perceived effort and reps-in-reserve.

Isometrics are not universally benign — high-intensity static holds with breath-holding can transiently spike blood pressure; the wall sit is gated behind clearance for uncontrolled hypertension or cardiac disease.
Resistance training alone is not a falls program — falls reduction requires balance and functional content.
Household-object training has thin object-specific trial evidence — it is justified by transfer from equipment-agnostic, home-delivery, and minimal-dose findings, not by trials of named objects.

setup: A stable, weight-bearing, immovable support (chair, counter, or wall)
Sealed or secured objects (check bottle caps; pack backpacks high and tight)
A chair within reach for every standing session
population_guards: Fragile track: balance/functional content is mandatory, not optional.
Hypertension / cardiac context: breathing cues and post-clearance gating for isometric holds.
Cardiac / high-risk: routed to clinical care, not enrolled in a tier.
All clinical conditions: adjunct-to-care language, never treatment claims.
Overweight track: function-not-weight framing; no diet or calorie prescriptions.
Wellbeing guard: users signalling disordered eating or compulsive exercise are routed away from intensity/volume escalation toward gentler, health-framed content and professional resources.
safety.note: Serious adverse events were rare across the cited resistance, falls, osteoarthritis, and cancer-survivor trials when programs are scaled appropriately; most reported events were transient muscle or joint soreness.

ui.signature: An offline, dependency-free SVG form-model engine: tempo-synced anatomical figures with the object drawn in place, front/side/mirror views, and a reduced-motion fallback.
principles: A figure is a small set of named joints; a movement is a short list of keyframe poses the engine interpolates between. Clean silhouette, not anatomical realism, so it reads instantly.
Tempo drives the animation clock, so the figure doubles as the pacer/metronome and the user moves with it; a down/hold/up phase indicator and optional audio ticks reinforce rhythm.
The object is part of the figure and tracks the relevant joint (bottles in hands, backpack on trunk, chair under hips) — users copy the setup, not just the pose.
Front/side/mirror toggles (side default for hinge and squat), synced cue lines, set-end RPE/RIR prompt, and a persistent red-flag affordance.

Accessibility is non-negotiable for this audience: respect prefers-reduced-motion with a static start/mid/end key-pose diagram and numbered cues; high-contrast strokes, large tap targets, larger-figure option, audio captions, keyboard operation.
ui.build_notes: Implement as a small vanilla-JS module beside the existing web/js views (e.g. formModel.js), consuming the same content JSON, rendering inline SVG, theming from existing tokens (--accent, --surface, --text, --ease, --sp-*). No framework, no animation library, no network. Reference engine: web/prototype/form-model.html.

tier_a: Screening onboarding tree (PAR-Q+-modelled triage, tier assignment, non-overridable professional routing)
Object library (the taxonomy as data; user marks owned objects to filter sessions)
Movement-pattern exercise library (pattern-tagged; default/regression/progression objects; cues; figure spec)
Guided session player (balanced cross-pattern sessions; per-exercise animation, tempo pacing, cueing, RPE/RIR check-ins)
Animated form-model engine (the signature UI feature; reduced-motion fallback)
tier_b: Autoregulation engine (RPE/RIR/talk-test intensity; one-variable-at-a-time progression; symptom-day deloads)
Balance-and-falls module (mandatory in the fragile track)
Exercise-snack mode (3-5 min sessions; break-up-your-sitting prompts for diabetes/sedentary users)
Home capacity checks (opt-in 30-second sit-to-stand and timed standing-balance; progress without a scale)
Progress surfacing (strength/stamina/balance/adherence trends, explicitly not weight)
tier_c: Condition adjunct packs (diabetes, hypertension with isometric guards, knee OA, cancer survivorship)
Object substitution suggester (no backpack? two filled bottles in a tote)
Source transparency panel (every benefit links to its bibliography entry)
Offline media packaging (figures/animations as bundled SVG)

sessions.note: Guided sessions (object library, movement-pattern exercise library, balanced session templates, and the animated form-model engine) are the next research deliverables. This page ships the evidence and taxonomy foundation that those build on. A working animation engine prototype ships at web/prototype/form-model.html.
planned: Balanced 12-15 min daily sessions drawing across squat/hinge/push/pull/carry/balance patterns
3-5 min exercise-snack micro-sessions and break-up-sitting prompts
Longer conditioning sessions for fitter users
Per-exercise animated figures with front/side/mirror views, the object in place, and tempo pacing
